learning_objectives: Understand the current indie game market and its economic realities
Identify viable niches and underserved audiences
Analyze the differences between solo development and small team structures
readings: Blood, Sweat, and Pixels Ch. 1-3 by Jason Schreier
State of Indie Gaming Report 2023 - Game Discoverability Now

lecture_notes.introduction: Indie game development is the art of doing more with less. This course teaches you to ship a complete, commercially viable game with the constraints of a solo developer or small team of 2-5 people.
key_concepts: The indie market in numbers: 14,000+ games released on Steam per year, median sales under 1,000 copies
Success factors: genre selection, audience targeting, timing, and marketing (not just game quality)
Solo developer vs. small team: when to do everything yourself vs. when to collaborate
The myth of the overnight success: most indie hits had years of prior work and failed projects
Financial planning: how long can you sustain development without revenue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Market analysis of three indie games released in the past year: one breakout hit, one moderate success, one commercial failure, examining what differentiated them
setup_requirements: SteamDB and SteamSpy access for market research
Game engine of choice installed (Unity, Godot, or Unreal)

review.value: The median indie game on Steam earns under $5,000 lifetime revenue. The top 1% earn over $1M. Genre selection and audience fit matter more than raw polish.
Solo: maximum creative control, slowest velocity. Duo: one programmer + one artist is the most common successful indie team structure. 3-5: requires project management overhead.

learning_objectives: Apply scope management techniques to keep projects viable for small teams
Build playable prototypes in under 48 hours to validate game concepts
Use the MoSCoW method and vertical slice approach for feature prioritization
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 8-9 by Jesse Schell
Extra Credits: Minimum Viable Product in Game Development (video)

lecture_notes.introduction: Scope creep has killed more indie games than bad code. The ability to ruthlessly prioritize features and validate ideas quickly is the single most important skill for an indie developer.
key_concepts: MoSCoW prioritization: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
The vertical slice: build one complete, polished slice of gameplay before expanding
48-hour prototype methodology: idea -> core loop -> playtest -> decide
Feature budgeting: estimating implementation time and assigning feature costs
Kill your darlings: recognizing when a feature you love doesn't serve the game
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live 30-minute rapid prototype of a game mechanic from concept to playable, demonstrating speed-focused development
setup_requirements: Game engine with hot reload capability
Whiteboard or digital canvas for feature mapping

review.value: One complete level or gameplay loop, fully polished with art, audio, and UI. If this slice is fun and shippable, the rest of the game is scaling horizontally.
Must: core gameplay loop. Should: quality-of-life features. Could: extra content and polish. Won't: features cut from this release entirely.

learning_objectives: Select art styles that are achievable for solo developers or small teams
Use procedural generation and asset reuse to stretch limited art resources
Implement visual cohesion through color palettes, lighting, and post-processing
readings: GDC Talk: The Art of Celeste - Achieving AAA Feel on an Indie Budget
Pixel Art Best Practices for Modern Games - Lospec Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: You do not need a team of 50 artists to make a beautiful game. Some of the most visually distinctive games in history were made by one or two people. The key is choosing a style that plays to your strengths and constraints.
key_concepts: Art styles ranked by production cost: abstract < pixel art < hand-drawn 2D < low-poly 3D < stylized 3D < realistic 3D
Coherence over fidelity: a consistent low-fidelity style looks better than inconsistent high-fidelity
Color theory for game art: limited palettes create visual identity and reduce production time
Procedural and modular art: tiles, atlases, and procedural generation to multiply a small asset library
Post-processing as a force multiplier: bloom, color grading, and screen-space effects that elevate simple art
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming a grey-boxed prototype level into a visually cohesive scene using only 16 colors, simple geometry, and post-processing
setup_requirements: Game engine with post-processing pipeline enabled
Aseprite or equivalent pixel art tool

review.value: Choose the cheapest style that serves your game's mood. Vampire Survivors uses simple sprite art. Superhot uses white/red minimalism. Both are visually iconic.
Consistent line weight, limited color palette, uniform lighting direction, matching level of detail across all assets, unified post-processing treatment.

learning_objectives: Evaluate whether crowdfunding is appropriate for a given project
Plan and execute a Kickstarter campaign with realistic goals and stretch goals
Understand alternative funding: publishers, grants, early access revenue, and government programs
readings: Kickstarter Game Campaign Post-Mortems Collection (curated list on course portal)
Thomas Brush: How I Funded My Game Without a Publisher

lecture_notes.introduction: Money is the most common reason indie games die before release. Understanding your funding options and choosing the right one for your project can mean the difference between shipping and abandoning.
key_concepts: Kickstarter mechanics: campaign page, tiers, stretch goals, backer management, fulfillment
Campaign math: Kickstarter takes 5%, payment processor takes 3-5%, fulfillment costs for physical rewards, taxes on income
The pre-campaign build-up: you need an audience before you launch, not after
Publisher deals: revenue split structures, milestone payments, creative control trade-offs
Alternative funding: government arts grants (Canada Media Fund, UK Games Fund), platform exclusivity deals, early access revenue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three Kickstarter campaigns: one that succeeded and delivered, one that succeeded and failed to deliver, one that failed to fund, examining what determined each outcome
setup_requirements: Kickstarter account for campaign page exploration
Portfolio of game assets for mock campaign creation

review.value: Average successful game Kickstarter raises $20K-50K. You need a playable demo, trailer, and existing audience of 1,000+ followers before launching. Campaign success rate for games is roughly 30%.
Publishers provide funding + marketing for 30-70% revenue share. Worth it if they bring genuine marketing reach. Not worth it if they just provide funding you could get elsewhere.

class_schedule.topic: Early Access Strategy and Community Feedback Loops
learning_objectives: Design an Early Access strategy that builds player trust and drives sales
Implement effective feedback collection and triage systems
Balance community requests against design vision and scope constraints
readings: Valve: Best Practices for Early Access on Steam
Slay the Spire Post-Mortem: How Early Access Shaped the Final Game

lecture_notes.introduction: Early Access is not an excuse to sell an unfinished game. It is a development methodology where paying players become your testing and feedback team. When done right, it is the most powerful tool in the indie developer's arsenal.
key_concepts: When to enter Early Access: the game must be fun and stable in its current state, not just functional
Pricing strategy: price at 80-100% of final price, reward early buyers with lower price, not a steep discount
Update cadence: commit to a schedule (biweekly or monthly) and stick to it, even if updates are small
Feedback triage: categorizing player feedback into bugs, balance issues, feature requests, and design disagreements
The vocal minority problem: Reddit and Discord amplify certain voices; use data alongside community sentiment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a structured feedback pipeline using Discord channels, a public roadmap (Trello/Notion), and in-game feedback tools
setup_requirements: Discord server with feedback channel structure
Trello or Notion board for public roadmap
Bug tracking system (GitHub Issues or Linear)

review.value: Game is fun in current state, 2+ hours of content, core loop is complete, save system works, no data-loss bugs, update plan communicated on store page.
Critical bugs: fix immediately. Balance issues: collect data before acting. Feature requests: evaluate against scope. Design disagreements: acknowledge but hold your vision.

class_schedule.topic: Post-Mortem Analysis and Sustainable Indie Careers
learning_objectives: Conduct a thorough post-mortem analysis of a completed game project
Identify patterns of success and failure across multiple indie post-mortems
Plan a sustainable career trajectory as an indie developer
readings: Gamasutra Post-Mortem Collection: Hades, Hollow Knight, Stardew Valley
Rami Ismail: How to Survive in Gamedev for Eleven Years

lecture_notes.introduction: Every shipped game, successful or not, contains lessons that make the next project better. The post-mortem is where those lessons are captured. A sustainable indie career is built on learning from each project and managing your energy and finances across multiple releases.
key_concepts: Post-mortem structure: what went right, what went wrong, what was surprising, actionable takeaways
Common failure patterns: scope creep, feature creep, marketing too late, burnout, running out of money
Common success patterns: strong vertical slice early, community building from day one, genre-audience fit
Financial sustainability: living expenses runway, revenue per title expectations, building a back catalog
Burnout prevention: sustainable work hours, taking breaks between projects, separating identity from product
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live post-mortem of the instructor's own shipped indie game, with honest financials and timeline analysis
setup_requirements: Financial tracking spreadsheet from course project

review.value: The most valuable post-mortems are honest about failures, specific about numbers (timeline, budget, sales), and result in concrete changes to the next project's approach.
Plan for 3-5 years to reach financial sustainability. Build a back catalog. Each game funds the next. Keep living expenses low during the ramp-up period.

questions.question: You are a solo developer planning a roguelike game. Your feature wishlist includes: procedural level generation, 50 unique enemies, 100 items, online leaderboards, local co-op, mod support, and a level editor. Using the MoSCoW method, categorize each feature and justify your categorization. Your target development time is 12 months.
questions.answer: Must have: procedural level generation (core genre requirement), 15-20 unique enemies (minimum for variety, reduced from 50). Should have: 30-50 items (enough for meaningful runs), online leaderboards (community engagement with relatively low implementation cost). Could have: local co-op (significant scope increase but high value), additional enemies up to 50 (if time permits). Won't have this release: mod support (massive engineering effort for a solo dev), level editor (enormous scope, can be post-launch if game succeeds). Justification: the core loop must be fun and complete first. Everything that doesn't directly serve a complete roguelike run is deprioritized. The enemy count and item count are reduced to achievable numbers that still provide variety.

rubric.key: All features categorized with MoSCoW labels
Justifications demonstrate realistic scope awareness for solo development
Reduced quantities for Must-have features to achievable numbers
Won't-have items are correctly identified as highest-risk scope additions

questions.question: Explain why a consistent low-fidelity art style (such as the pixel art in Celeste or the paper cutout style of Baba Is You) often looks better in a finished game than a mix of high-quality assets from different sources. Include at least two specific visual coherence principles in your answer.
questions.answer: Visual coherence creates a unified aesthetic identity that players read as intentional and polished, even at low fidelity. Two key principles: (1) Consistent line weight and detail density - when all sprites have the same pixel resolution or line thickness, the eye accepts the style as a deliberate choice. Mixing a detailed 64x64 sprite with a rough 16x16 sprite breaks the illusion. (2) Unified color palette - using a limited, curated palette (e.g., 16-32 colors) forces all assets to share the same color temperature and saturation, creating visual harmony. A single asset store character with 1000 unique colors will look alien next to hand-crafted pixel art using a restricted palette. Additional factors include matching lighting direction across all assets and maintaining consistent proportions and scale across characters, objects, and environments.

rubric.key: Explains why coherence reads as polish regardless of fidelity
Identifies consistent line weight or detail density as a principle
Identifies unified color palette as a principle
Provides concrete examples from real games

quizzes.topic: Funding, Early Access, and Community Management

questions.question: Your indie game has been in Early Access for 3 months. You have 2,000 players and a 78% positive review rating. The most common negative review complaint is that the game lacks content (only 3 hours of gameplay). The most requested community feature is online multiplayer, which would take 4 months to implement. Your funding runway is 6 months. What is your strategic plan for the next 6 months? Justify each decision.
questions.answer: Month 1-2: Address the content complaint directly. Add 2-3 new levels, new enemy types, and a new game mechanic using existing systems. This targets the primary negative review driver without massive engineering effort. Do NOT build multiplayer. Month 3: Release a major content update to trigger Steam's algorithm boost for updated games. Coordinate with community and content creators for coverage. Month 4-5: Continue content additions and polish based on community feedback. Begin planning 1.0 launch. Month 5-6: Prepare and execute 1.0 full launch with launch discount and press campaign. Justification for not building multiplayer: 4 months of development for multiplayer consumes the entire remaining runway with no guarantee of return. The negative reviews cite content, not missing multiplayer. Adding content is lower risk, directly addresses reviews, and preserves runway for a strong 1.0 launch. Multiplayer can be a post-launch addition if 1.0 revenue supports continued development.

rubric.key: Addresses the actual problem (content length) rather than the loudest request (multiplayer)
Financial reasoning connects decisions to the 6-month runway constraint
Includes a plan for 1.0 launch within the runway period
Acknowledges multiplayer as a potential post-launch addition rather than dismissing it

questions.question: You are planning a Kickstarter campaign for your indie game. Your development budget is $40,000. Calculate the minimum funding goal you need to set on Kickstarter to actually receive $40,000 after all platform fees and estimated taxes, showing your work. Then explain three things you must have in place before launching the campaign.
questions.answer: Calculation: Kickstarter takes 5% platform fee. Payment processing (Stripe) takes approximately 3% + $0.20 per pledge. Assuming average pledge of $25, the per-pledge processing fee is roughly 3.8%. Total platform fees: approximately 8.8%. To receive $40,000: Goal = $40,000 / (1 - 0.088) = $40,000 / 0.912 = approximately $43,860. After income tax (estimated 20-25% depending on jurisdiction), you need even more: approximately $54,825 to net $40,000 after fees and taxes. Set goal at minimum $44,000 for post-fee revenue, but target $55,000 to account for taxes. Three pre-launch requirements: (1) A playable demo that potential backers can try, proving the game is fun and the team can execute. (2) An existing audience of at least 1,000 followers across social media and email list, because Kickstarter does not provide discoverability and most pledges come from your own audience on day one. (3) A professional-quality trailer that has been tested with audiences outside your friend group, as the trailer is the primary conversion tool on the campaign page.

rubric.key: Correct platform fee calculation (5% Kickstarter + ~3-4% payment processing)
Accounts for taxes as a significant additional cost
Final goal number is mathematically sound
Three pre-launch requirements are specific and well-justified

major_assignments.title: Indie Game Vertical Slice
major_assignments.description: Build a complete vertical slice of an original indie game concept. The vertical slice must represent 10-15 minutes of polished, playable content that demonstrates the core gameplay loop, art direction, and audio design. This slice must be of sufficient quality to serve as a demo for a hypothetical store page or crowdfunding campaign.
objectives: Demonstrate scope management by delivering a polished slice rather than a sprawling prototype
Create a cohesive art style achievable by a solo developer or small team
Implement a complete core gameplay loop that is fun and replayable
Produce a playable artifact suitable for market validation
deliverables: Playable game build (Windows and/or web export)
Source project files with clean, documented code
One-page design document describing the full game vision and how this slice represents it
Art style guide showing the visual constraints and palette choices
Development time log showing actual hours spent per feature category
60-second gameplay trailer captured from the vertical slice

criteria: Core gameplay loop is complete and fun
Controls are responsive and intuitive
Difficulty is appropriate and intentionally tuned
The slice demonstrates a clear unique selling point
Art style is consistent across all assets
Color palette is deliberate and limited
Visual feedback for player actions is clear
Style is achievable for the stated team size
Slice is appropriately scoped for the timeline
No placeholder or unfinished features visible to the player
Time log demonstrates efficient allocation of effort
Design document shows realistic full game scope
Game has a title screen and clean exit flow
Audio is present (music and/or sound effects)
No crashes or game-breaking bugs
Trailer effectively showcases the gameplay
Complete and polished vertical slice with cohesive art and gameplay
Demonstrates effective scope management within timeline constraints
Suitable for use as a playable demo or campaign artifact
Accompanied by professional documentation and trailer

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of indie development concepts and strategic decision-making

rapid_prototypes.description: Weekly 48-hour prototype exercises demonstrating rapid development skills

vertical_slice_project.description: Complete vertical slice demonstrating scope management, art direction, and gameplay quality

marketing_materials.description: Store page mockup, trailer, and crowdfunding campaign plan for the vertical slice

post_mortem_analysis.description: Written post-mortem of the vertical slice project with honest assessment and actionable learnings

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for playtest sessions and prototype reviews. Remote attendance is permitted for lecture sessions with prior arrangement. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Prototype exercises have hard deadlines (48 hours from assignment) with no late submissions accepted, simulating game jam conditions.
course_policies.collaboration: The vertical slice project may be completed solo or in pairs. If working in pairs, each member must contribute to both code and art/design and submit individual reflection documents. All other assignments are individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Asset store purchases and free assets are permitted if they match your art style and are credited. Code must be original or open-source with attribution. Using AI art generation tools is permitted but must be disclosed and must still achieve visual coherence.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students may use any game engine (Unity, Godot, Unreal, GameMaker, or custom). The engine choice must be justified in the design document. Students must be able to produce standalone builds for playtesting.
